title_en: The Promise of Ease After Hardship
content_en:
One of the most frequently cited passages in the Quran for believers facing difficulty is the repeated promise in Surah Ash-Sharh: 'Fa inna ma'al usri yusra, inna ma'al usri yusra' — 'For indeed, with hardship comes ease; indeed, with hardship comes ease.' The repetition of this promise in consecutive verses is not mere literary emphasis. Ibn Taymiyyah and other scholars of Quranic exegesis note a significant grammatical point: the word 'hardship' (al-usr) is definite (with the article al-), meaning it refers to the same hardship, while 'ease' (yusr) is indefinite, suggesting that multiple forms of ease accompany a single hardship. The tradition records: 'One hardship will never overcome two eases.'

This Quranic promise is not merely consolation but a statement of cosmic law embedded in the nature of divine creation. Hardship and ease, difficulty and relief, are bound together as inseparable companions in the divine plan — not as remote sequential possibilities but as simultaneous realities. The ease already accompanies the hardship even before the hardship has visibly lifted. The person facing distress is already inside the promise, even when they cannot yet perceive its fulfillment.

Ibn Taymiyyah connects this promise to the broader pattern of prophetic experience. Every prophet faced extraordinary hardship before relief came. Ibrahim was cast into fire and saved. Yusuf (Joseph) was thrown into a well, enslaved, and imprisoned before ascending to the throne of Egypt. Musa fled for his life from Pharaoh before leading his people to freedom. The Prophet Muhammad endured years of persecution, the death of loved ones, boycotts, and exile before the triumph of Makkah's opening. The pattern is consistent: the depth of the trial often corresponds to the magnitude of the relief and elevation that follows.

For the believer personally, this promise generates a specific spiritual discipline: not to make permanent decisions based on the temporary state of hardship. The person who abandons faith, community, or moral principles because of a season of difficulty has acted on a temporary condition as if it were permanent — which the Quran's promise makes clear it is not. Patience, therefore, is not merely a virtue for its own sake but a practical discipline aligned with the actual nature of reality.

The surah also reminds the Prophet — and through him, all believers — that relief does not mean passive waiting. The command following the promise is: 'So when you have finished, then stand up for worship, and to your Lord direct your longing.' The end of one difficulty should be immediately redirected toward further worship and aspiration toward Allah, not toward exhausted passivity. Relief from distress, in the Islamic framework, is not the destination but a renewed beginning — an opportunity to deepen one's servitude, express gratitude, and continue the journey toward the ultimate and permanent ease of Paradise.
content_id:
Salah satu kutipan Al-Qur'an yang paling sering dirujuk oleh orang-orang beriman yang menghadapi kesulitan adalah janji yang diulang dalam Surah Asy-Syarh: Fa inna ma'al usri yusra, inna ma'al usri yusra — Karena sesungguhnya bersama kesulitan ada kemudahan, sesungguhnya bersama kesulitan ada kemudahan. Pengulangan janji ini dalam ayat-ayat yang berurutan bukan sekadar penekanan sastra. Ibnu Taimiyyah dan ulama-ulama tafsir Al-Qur'an lainnya mencatat poin gramatikal yang signifikan: kata kesulitan (al-usr) bersifat definitif (dengan artikel al-), berarti merujuk pada kesulitan yang sama, sementara kemudahan (yusr) bersifat indefinitif, menunjukkan bahwa berbagai bentuk kemudahan menyertai satu kesulitan. Tradisi mencatat: Satu kesulitan tidak akan pernah mengalahkan dua kemudahan.

Janji Al-Qur'an ini bukan sekadar penghiburan tetapi sebuah pernyataan hukum kosmik yang tertanam dalam sifat ciptaan Ilahi. Kesulitan dan kemudahan, kesukaran dan keringanan, terikat bersama sebagai teman tak terpisahkan dalam rencana Ilahi — bukan sebagai kemungkinan sekuensial yang jauh melainkan sebagai realitas yang bersamaan. Kemudahan itu sudah menyertai kesulitan bahkan sebelum kesulitan secara nyata terangkat. Orang yang menghadapi kesulitan sudah berada di dalam janji, meskipun mereka belum dapat merasakan pemenuhannya.

Ibnu Taimiyyah menghubungkan janji ini dengan pola pengalaman kenabian yang lebih luas. Setiap nabi menghadapi kesulitan luar biasa sebelum keringanan datang. Ibrahim dilempar ke dalam api dan diselamatkan. Yusuf (Yusuf) dilempar ke dalam sumur, diperbudak, dan dipenjara sebelum naik ke tahta Mesir. Musa melarikan diri menyelamatkan nyawanya dari Fir'aun sebelum memimpin kaumnya menuju kemerdekaan. Nabi Muhammad menanggung bertahun-tahun penganiayaan, kematian orang-orang yang dicintai, pemboikotan, dan pengasingan sebelum kemenangan Pembukaan Makkah. Polanya konsisten: kedalaman ujian sering kali sesuai dengan besarnya keringanan dan ketinggian yang mengikutinya.

Bagi orang beriman secara pribadi, janji ini menghasilkan disiplin spiritual yang spesifik: tidak membuat keputusan permanen berdasarkan keadaan kesulitan yang sementara. Orang yang meninggalkan keimanan, komunitas, atau prinsip-prinsip moral karena musim kesulitan telah bertindak atas kondisi sementara seolah-olah itu permanen — yang janji Al-Qur'an dengan jelas menyatakan bahwa itu bukanlah demikian. Kesabaran, oleh karena itu, bukan sekadar kebajikan demi dirinya sendiri tetapi sebuah disiplin praktis yang selaras dengan sifat aktual realitas.

Surah itu juga mengingatkan Nabi — dan melalui beliau, semua orang beriman — bahwa keringanan tidak berarti menunggu secara pasif. Perintah yang mengikuti janji adalah: Maka apabila kamu telah selesai, maka tetaplah bekerja keras. Dan hanya kepada Tuhanmulah engkau berharap. Akhir dari satu kesulitan harus segera diarahkan kembali kepada ibadah dan aspirasi selanjutnya kepada Allah, bukan kepada kepasifan yang kelelahan. Keringanan dari kesulitan, dalam kerangka Islam, bukanlah tujuan akhir melainkan awal yang diperbarui — sebuah kesempatan untuk memperdalam penghambaan seseorang, mengungkapkan rasa syukur, dan melanjutkan perjalanan menuju kemudahan yang abadi dan permanen dari Surga.
content_ar: One of the most frequently cited passages in the القرآن الكريم for المؤمنون facing difficulty is the repeated promise in السورة Ash-Sharh: 'Fa inna ma'al usri yusra, inna ma'al usri yusra' — 'For indeed, with الشدة comes اليسر; indeed, with الشدة comes اليسر.' The repetition of this promise in consecutive الآيات is not mere literary emphasis. ابن تيمية and other العلماء of القرآني exegesis note a المهم grammatical point: the word 'الشدة' (al-usr) is definite (with the article al-), meaning it refers to the same الشدة, بينما 'اليسر' (yusr) is indefinite, suggesting that multiple forms of اليسر accompany a single الشدة. The التراث records: 'One الشدة will never overcome two eases.' This القرآني promise is not merely consolation but a statement of cosmic law embedded in the nature of divine creation. الشدة and اليسر, difficulty and الفرج, are bound together as inseparable الصحابة in the divine plan — not as remote sequential possibilities but as simultaneous realities. The اليسر already accompanies the الشدة even قبل the الشدة has visibly lifted. The person facing distress is already inside the promise, even عندما they cannot yet perceive its fulfillment. ابن تيمية connects this promise to the broader pattern of prophetic experience. Every النبي faced extraordinary الشدة قبل الفرج came. إبراهيم ﷺ was cast into fire and saved. يوسف ﷺ (يوسف) was thrown into a well, enslaved, and imprisoned قبل ascending to the throne of Egypt. موسى ﷺ fled for his الحياة from Pharaoh قبل leading his people to freedom. النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ endured years of persecution, the الموت of loved ones, boycotts, and exile قبل the triumph of مكة المكرمة's opening. The pattern is consistent: the depth of the الابتلاء often corresponds to the magnitude of the الفرج and elevation that follows. For the المؤمن personally, this promise generates a specific spiritual discipline: not to make permanent decisions based on the temporary state of الشدة. The person who abandons الإيمان, الجماعة, or moral principles لأن of a season of difficulty has acted on a temporary condition as إذا it were permanent — which the القرآن الكريم's promise makes clear it is not. الصبر, لذلك, is not merely a الفضيلة for its own sake but a practical discipline aligned with the actual nature of reality. The السورة أيضاً reminds النبي ﷺ — and through him, all المؤمنون — that الفرج does not mean passive waiting. The command following the promise is: 'لذا عندما you have finished, then stand up for worship, and to your Lord direct your longing.' The end of one difficulty should be immediately redirected toward further worship and aspiration toward الله, not toward exhausted passivity. الفرج from distress, in the Islamic framework, is not the destination but a renewed beginning — an opportunity to deepen one's servitude, express الشكر, and continue the journey toward the ultimate and permanent اليسر of الجنة.
